explanation: For patients relapsing post-allogeneic stem cell transplant with mixed chimerism and no active, uncontrolled GVHD, the goal is to re-establish donor chimerism and trigger a graft-versus-leukemia (GVL) effect using Donor Lymphocyte Infusion (DLI), often preceded by cytoreductive targeted salvage or low-dose chemotherapy (such as Azacitidine) to lower the tumor burden. Discontinuing immunosuppression alone (Option D) is dangerous if not done systematically and is rarely sufficient as a single measure for frank leukemic relapse. A second transplant (Option A) has high treatment-related mortality and is reserved for select cases that fail DLI. High-dose chemotherapy alone (Option C) has very poor long-term efficacy post-transplant relapse without immunological intervention.
takeaway: Management of post-transplant AML relapse utilizes donor lymphocyte infusions (DLI), often combined with hypomethylating agents, to exploit the immunotherapeutic graft-versus-leukemia effect.

title: Post-Remission Oral Maintenance
vignette: A 67-year-old male achieved complete remission (CR) with standard induction and 2 cycles of intermediate-dose cytarabine consolidation for de novo AML. He is not considered a candidate for allogeneic stem cell transplant due to age and patient preference. Which maintenance therapy has demonstrated a significant overall survival benefit in this population?
options: Maintenance therapy is not indicated; observation is the standard of care.
correct_answer: B. Oral Azacitidine (CC-486)
explanation: The phase 3 QUAZAR AML-001 trial showed that oral Azacitidine (CC-486) as maintenance therapy significantly prolonged overall survival (24.7 months vs 14.8 months) and relapse-free survival compared to placebo in patients aged 55 years or older with AML in first complete remission (CR) or complete remission with incomplete blood count recovery (CRi) after intensive chemotherapy, who were not candidates for or chose not to undergo allogeneic stem cell transplant. CC-486 has a distinct pharmacokinetic profile and is not interchangeable with injectable Azacitidine (Option C or other formulations). Observation (Option E) was the former standard, but oral Azacitidine maintenance is now recommended.
takeaway: Oral Azacitidine (CC-486) is the first maintenance therapy to show a significant overall survival benefit for older AML patients in complete remission who are not candidates for consolidative stem cell transplant.

title: Pharmacokinetic Drug Interactions (Venetoclax / Posaconazole)
vignette: A 71-year-old male with newly diagnosed AML is treated with Azacitidine and Venetoclax. On day 5 of therapy, he is diagnosed with a severe invasive Aspergillus pulmonary infection and is started on therapeutic oral Posaconazole (a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor). What dosage adjustment is mandatory for Venetoclax to prevent severe, prolonged myelosuppression?
options: No adjustment is required; Venetoclax is not metabolized by the liver
Reduce the Venetoclax dose by approximately 75 percent (specifically 75%, e.g., from 400 mg to 100 mg daily)
Increase the Venetoclax dose to overcome competitive enzyme inhibition
Discontinue Azacitidine immediately but keep Venetoclax at full dose
Double the dose of Posaconazole to clear Venetoclax faster
correct_answer: B. Reduce the Venetoclax dose by approximately 75 percent (specifically 75%, e.g., from 400 mg to 100 mg daily)
explanation: Venetoclax is primarily metabolized by the cytochrome P450 enzyme CYP3A4. Concomitant administration of strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (such as Posaconazole, Voriconazole, Ketoconazole, or Clarithromycin) dramatically increases Venetoclax plasma concentration (up to 4-fold), posing an extreme risk of life-threatening tumor lysis syndrome, severe neutropenia, and thrombocytopenia. Product guidelines mandate a 75 percent (specifically 75%) dose reduction of Venetoclax (reducing a standard 400 mg dose to 100 mg daily, or a 100 mg dose to 50 mg/20 mg depending on the clinical target) when co-administered with a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor.
takeaway: Co-administration of Venetoclax with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (like Posaconazole) requires a mandatory 75 percent (specifically 75%) dose reduction of Venetoclax to avoid severe, toxic myelosuppression.

title: Relapsed APL Salvage
vignette: A 34-year-old male presents with relapsed Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia (APL) 18 months after completing first-line therapy with All-Trans Retinoic Acid (ATRA) and Anthracycline-based chemotherapy (LPA99 protocol). Molecular testing confirms PML-RARA positivity. What is the preferred salvage induction therapy?
options: Re-induction with ATRA and Anthracycline-based chemotherapy (Idarubicin)
Arsenic Trioxide (ATO) with or without ATRA
Allogeneic stem cell transplant immediately without inducing second remission
correct_answer: B. Arsenic Trioxide (ATO) with or without ATRA
explanation: For patients relapsing after first-line APL therapy who have not previously received Arsenic Trioxide (ATO), salvage therapy with Arsenic Trioxide (ATO), with or without ATRA, is highly effective, inducing complete molecular remission in over 85 percent (specifically 85%) of cases. Once a second molecular remission is achieved, consolidation can be followed by autologous stem cell transplant (or allogeneic transplant in patients with early relapse or persistent molecular positivity). Standard de novo chemotherapy (Option C) is less effective and carries unnecessary cardiotoxic risks. Immediate allogeneic transplant (Option E) should not be performed in active, uncontrolled relapse.
takeaway: Arsenic Trioxide (ATO) is the salvage therapy of choice for patients with relapsed APL who have not previously been exposed to ATO.

title: TP53 mutated secondary MDS Transformation
vignette: A 69-year-old male with a history of Myelodysplastic Syndrome (MDS) presents with transformation to Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) with 32 percent blasts in the bone marrow. Mutational testing reveals a TP53 mutation and cytogenetics reveal a complex monosomal karyotype. He is not a candidate for allogeneic stem cell transplantation. What is the standard non-intensive regimen associated with the best balance of tolerability and efficacy in this molecular subtype?
options: Decitabine (10-day regimen) or Azacitidine combined with Venetoclax
Gemtuzumab Ozogamicin combined with low-dose cytarabine
correct_answer: C. Decitabine (10-day regimen) or Azacitidine combined with Venetoclax
explanation: For older patients with secondary AML (arising from MDS) or TP53-mutated AML who are unfit for intensive options like transplantation or CPX-351 (which is only beneficial if a transplant is planned), non-intensive hypomethylating agent-based therapies are preferred. Decitabine (often given as a 10-day regimen) or Azacitidine combined with Venetoclax provides a reasonable balance of tolerability and response, though TP53-mutated disease remains highly resistant. CPX-351 (Option B) is intensive and associated with prolonged cytopenias; it is not recommended if the patient is not a candidate for a subsequent consolidative allogeneic stem cell transplant.
takeaway: For unfit patients with TP53-mutated secondary AML, non-intensive hypomethylating agent-based therapy (such as Azacitidine and Venetoclax) remains the standard treatment.

title: Anthracycline-Induced LVEF Drops
vignette: A 48-year-old de novo AML patient completes induction chemotherapy with 7+3 (daunorubicin at 60 mg/m2 daily for 3 days). He is scheduled for 3 cycles of consolidation therapy using Intermediate-Dose Cytarabine. During his pre-consolidation assessment, his baseline echocardiogram shows a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 42% (baseline was 55% before induction). He is completely asymptomatic.
options: Proceed with consolidative cytarabine as planned; cytarabine has no significant cardiotoxicity
Administer consolidative cytarabine but switch daunorubicin to idarubicin for consolidation
Halt all therapy and start high-dose beta-blockers immediately
Proceed with consolidation, but add Gemtuzumab Ozogamicin to protect the heart
Repeat induction chemotherapy with standard dose daunorubicin
correct_answer: A. Proceed with consolidative cytarabine as planned; cytarabine has no significant cardiotoxicity
explanation: Intermediate-to-high-dose Cytarabine (the standard consolidation agent in AML) does not carry significant myocardial toxicity or risk of exacerbating anthracycline-induced left ventricular dysfunction. Therefore, consolidative cytarabine can be safely administered as planned despite the drop in LVEF. Further anthracyclines (such as daunorubicin or idarubicin) must be avoided, as cumulative anthracycline exposure is the primary driver of myocardial damage (Option B is incorrect). Switch to supportive cardiac care (Option C) is not required to halt cytarabine-based consolidation.
takeaway: Cytarabine consolidation does not cause myocardial damage and can be safely administered even in patients experiencing anthracycline-induced drops in left ventricular ejection fraction.

title: AML-001 (Board Review Set 1)
vignette: A 42-year-old woman presents with bruising and gum bleeding. FBC shows Hb 82 g/L, WBC 28 x 109/L, platelets 18 x 109/L. Blood film shows abnormal promyelocytes with heavy granulation and bundles of Auer rods. Coagulation screen shows prolonged PT, prolonged APTT, low fibrinogen and high D-dimer. What is the most important immediate treatment?
correct_answer: B. All-trans retinoic acid
explanation: This is acute promyelocytic leukaemia. ATRA should be started immediately because early death is commonly due to DIC-related bleeding.
takeaway: Suspected APL = start ATRA first, confirm genetics after.

title: AML-002 (Board Review Set 1)
vignette: A 55-year-old man has AML. Cytogenetics shows t(8;21)(q22;q22). Which fusion gene is expected?
correct_answer: B. RUNX1::RUNX1T1
explanation: t(8;21) produces RUNX1::RUNX1T1 and is a core-binding factor AML.
takeaway: t(8;21) and inv(16) are classic favourable-risk AML groups.

title: AML-003 (Board Review Set 1)
vignette: A marrow aspirate shows 16% blasts with multilineage dysplasia. There is no AML-defining genetic abnormality. What is the best diagnosis?
correct_answer: B. MDS with excess blasts
explanation: Without AML-defining genetics, AML generally requires >=20% blasts. Sixteen percent blasts with dysplasia fits MDS with excess blasts.
takeaway: Always check the blast percentage before calling AML.

title: AML-004 (Board Review Set 1)
vignette: A patient with AML has NPM1 mutation and no FLT3-ITD. What is the usual ELN risk group?
correct_answer: A. Favourable
explanation: NPM1-mutated AML without FLT3-ITD is generally favourable risk.
takeaway: NPM1 must be interpreted together with FLT3 status.

title: AML-005 (Board Review Set 1)
vignette: Which flow cytometry pattern best supports AML?
correct_answer: C. CD13+, CD33+, MPO+
explanation: CD13, CD33 and MPO support myeloid lineage.
takeaway: MPO positivity is a strong marker of myeloid differentiation.

title: AML-006 (Board Review Set 1)
vignette: A patient with AML has inv(16)(p13q22). Which feature is most associated with this subtype?
correct_answer: B. Abnormal eosinophils
explanation: inv(16) produces CBFB::MYH11 and is associated with abnormal eosinophils.
takeaway: AML with eosinophilia should make you think of inv(16).

title: AML-007 (Board Review Set 1)
vignette: A 68-year-old patient is newly diagnosed with AML but is unfit for intensive chemotherapy. Which is the most appropriate treatment approach?
correct_answer: B. Azacitidine plus venetoclax
explanation: Older or unfit patients are commonly treated with hypomethylating agent plus venetoclax.
takeaway: AML treatment starts with fitness assessment.

title: AML-008 (Board Review Set 1)
vignette: Which genetic abnormality is strongly associated with adverse-risk AML?
correct_answer: D. TP53 mutation
explanation: TP53-mutated AML is associated with poor response, complex karyotype and adverse prognosis.
takeaway: TP53 AML behaves badly even when morphology looks ordinary.

title: AML-009 (Board Review Set 1)
vignette: A patient has AML with WBC 180 x 109/L, headache, confusion and hypoxia. What complication is most likely?
correct_answer: B. Leukostasis
explanation: Hyperleukocytosis with respiratory or neurological symptoms suggests leukostasis.
takeaway: Leukostasis is a clinical emergency, not just a high WBC number.

title: AML-010 (Board Review Set 1)
vignette: A blood film shows neutrophilia, toxic granulation and Döhle bodies, but no blasts. What is the most likely diagnosis?
correct_answer: B. Reactive neutrophilia
explanation: Toxic granulation and Döhle bodies are reactive changes, commonly seen in infection.
takeaway: Do not diagnose AML without blasts or supportive marrow/flow evidence.
